questionText: A toy robot play mat is shaped like an L. The top side is 13 cm, the left side is 9 cm, the bottom short side is 5 cm, and the right short side is 4 cm. Work out the two missing inside sides, then find the perimeter of the L-shape.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
explanation: First find the missing inside sides. The inside horizontal side is 13 - 5 = 8 cm because the full top length is 13 cm and the bottom short part is 5 cm. The inside vertical side is 9 - 4 = 5 cm because the full left height is 9 cm and the right short side is 4 cm. Now add all 6 outer edges of the L-shape: 13 + 4 + 8 + 5 + 5 + 9 = 44 cm. So the perimeter is 44 cm. The other options are too small or too large because they do not include all six sides correctly.
generationMetadata: {"questionNumber":"Q1","questionType":"mcq","subject":"Maths","grade":4,"topic":"Measurement & Conversion","subtopic":"Perimeter & Area","learning_objective":"Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes","focus":"multi_step_application"}

questionText: Choose the sentence that is correct about a toy track shaped like an L. It starts as a 13 m by 9 m rectangle, and then a 5 m by 3 m corner is cut away. One claim says the new perimeter stays 44 m because the 5 m and 3 m that were cut off are replaced by two new sides of 5 m and 3 m. Another claim says the new perimeter is 36 m because some area was removed. Pick the correct sentence.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
options: [{"text":"The first claim is correct. The perimeter stays 44 m because the removed outer lengths are replaced by equal new inner lengths.","correct":true},{"text":"The second claim is correct. The perimeter becomes 36 m because cutting away a corner always makes the perimeter shorter.","correct":false},{"text":"Both claims are wrong. The perimeter becomes 52 m because you add 5 m and 3 m to 44 m.","correct":false},{"text":"Both claims are wrong. The perimeter becomes 41 m because 44 m minus 3 m is 41 m.","correct":false}]
explanation: Nice thinking! The rectangle’s perimeter is 2 × (13 + 9) = 44 m. Cutting out a corner removes 5 m and 3 m, but it also makes two new sides of 5 m and 3 m, so the perimeter stays 44 m.

questionText: A sticker path is a closed rectilinear shape. Going around it in order, the sides are: right 16 cm, down 5 cm, left 6 cm, down 4 cm, left 10 cm, and then up one side back to the start. The whole perimeter is 50 cm. Choose the correct answer about the last side and the path.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
options: [{"text":"The last side is 9 cm, and the path closes because 5 cm + 4 cm = 9 cm.","correct":true},{"text":"The last side is 10 cm, because 50 - 16 - 5 - 6 - 4 - 10 = 10.","correct":false},{"text":"The last side is 11 cm, because the two left moves make 16 cm and then 11 cm finishes the shape.","correct":false},{"text":"The last side is 7 cm, because 16 - 6 - 10 = 0, so only 7 cm is needed.","correct":false}]
explanation: Great job checking both facts! The missing side from the perimeter is 50 - (16 + 5 + 6 + 4 + 10) = 9 cm. It also makes sense because the shape goes down 5 cm and down 4 cm, so it must go up 9 cm to close.
generationMetadata: {"questionNumber":"Q3","questionType":"mcq","subject":"Maths","grade":4,"topic":"Measurement & Conversion","subtopic":"Perimeter & Area","learning_objective":"Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes","focus":"reverse_reasoning"}

questionText: Look at two ways to work out the border of a snack maze. The shape starts as a 18 m by 11 m rectangle. Then a rectangular notch 4 m wide and 3 m deep is cut from the top edge, not from a corner. Method A says the perimeter is still 58 m because the top 4 m that disappeared is replaced by another 4 m at the bottom of the notch. Method B says the perimeter is 64 m because the notch adds two extra sides of 3 m. Pick the best judgment.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
options: [{"text":"Method A is correct, because the 4 m across the notch replaces the 4 m removed, so nothing changes.","correct":false},{"text":"Method B is correct, because the rectangle was 58 m and the notch adds 3 m + 3 m more, making 64 m.","correct":true},{"text":"Both methods are wrong. The perimeter is 61 m because only one extra side of 3 m should be added.","correct":false},{"text":"Both methods are right, because 58 m and 64 m can both describe the same shape.","correct":false}]
explanation: Well done! The rectangle perimeter is 2 × (18 + 11) = 58 m. The 4 m removed from the top is replaced by 4 m across the notch, so those cancel. But the notch adds two new vertical sides, 3 m and 3 m, so the new perimeter is 58 + 6 = 64 m.

questionText: A pet house wall is an L-shape with perimeter 64 cm. Its top side is 18 cm, left side is 14 cm, bottom short side is 7 cm, and one inside vertical side is 6 cm. What is the missing right short side?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
explanation: First find the inside horizontal side: 18 − 7 = 11 cm. In this L-shape, the full left height equals the two vertical parts on the right, so right short side + 6 = 14. Therefore the missing right short side is 14 − 6 = 8 cm. Check with the perimeter: 18 + 8 + 11 + 6 + 7 + 14 = 64 cm, so 8 cm is correct. The other options do not satisfy both the side relationship and the total perimeter.

questionText: A candy maze is made from a 20 cm by 11 cm rectangle with a notch 4 cm wide and 5 cm deep cut from the bottom middle. Which claim correctly explains the perimeter?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
options: [{"text":"The perimeter is 62 cm because the notch adds 5 cm twice.","correct":false},{"text":"The perimeter is 58 cm because the notch width is 4 cm.","correct":false},{"text":"The perimeter is 54 cm because that is the rectangle perimeter.","correct":false},{"text":"The perimeter is 72 cm because the rectangle perimeter is 62 cm and the notch adds two extra 5 cm sides.","correct":true}]
explanation: The rectangle perimeter is 2 × (20 + 11) = 62 cm. The 4 cm width removed from the bottom is replaced by the 4 cm inside part of the notch, so the width does not change the total perimeter. The notch adds two new vertical sides of 5 cm each, which adds 10 cm. So the new perimeter is 62 + 10 = 72 cm.

questionText: A toy road shape is a closed rectilinear path. Going around the boundary in order, the sides are: right 14 m, down 4 m, left 3 m, down 6 m, left 7 m, down 2 m, left 4 m, and then one final side back to the starting point. What is the length of the missing side?

learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
explanation: Add the horizontal moves: 14 m to the right, then 3 m + 7 m + 4 m = 14 m to the left, so the horizontal movement balances. For the vertical moves, the path goes down 4 m + 6 m + 2 m = 12 m in total. To return to the start, the final missing side must go up 12 m. So the missing side is 12 m.

options: [{"text":"2 × (16 + 13) and 16 + 9 + 11 + 4 + 5 + 13 both equal 58 m.","correct":true},{"text":"2 × (16 + 13) is smaller than 16 + 9 + 11 + 4 + 5 + 13 by 9 m.","correct":false},{"text":"16 + 9 + 11 + 4 + 5 + 13 is larger than 2 × (16 + 13) by 4 m.","correct":false},{"text":"The two methods cannot match because one uses six sides and the other uses four.","correct":false}]
explanation: The rectangle perimeter is 2 × (16 + 13) = 58 m. After the 5 m by 4 m corner is cut out, the new inside sides are 16 − 5 = 11 m and 13 − 4 = 9 m. questionText: A playground map is a U-shape made from a 19 m by 10 m rectangle with a top notch 5 m deep. The perimeter is said to be 64 m. Which statement is correct?

options: [{"text":"No notch width works, because the perimeter is always 2 × (19 + 10) + 5 + 5 = 68 m.","correct":true},{"text":"The notch width must be 6 m because 2 × (19 + 10) + notch width = 64.","correct":false},{"text":"Any notch width works because only the depth matters and 64 m is possible.","correct":false},{"text":"The notch width must be 5 m because the depth is 5 m.","correct":false}]
explanation: The rectangle perimeter is 2 × (19 + 10) = 58 m. For a top notch, the removed notch width is replaced by the bottom of the notch, so the width cancels out. questionText: A giant cookie path is made from a 14 cm by 12 cm rectangle. A rectangular notch 3 cm wide and 4 cm deep is cut from the left side. Which statement correctly gives the perimeter of the new shape?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
options: [{"text":"52 cm, because the 4 cm side removed is replaced by another 4 cm side, so only two extra 3 cm edges change the perimeter.","correct":false},{"text":"56 cm, because cutting a notch adds only one extra 4 cm side to the rectangle perimeter.","correct":false},{"text":"58 cm, because the original perimeter is 52 cm and the notch adds two extra 3 cm horizontal edges, for 52 + 6 = 58 cm.","correct":true},{"text":"66 cm, because both the notch width and depth should all be added twice: 52 + 2×3 + 2×4.","correct":false}]
explanation:
First find the rectangle perimeter: 2 × (14 + 12) = 52 cm. Cutting a notch 3 cm wide and 4 cm deep removes one outside vertical part of 4 cm, but the notch creates a new inside vertical side of 4 cm, so those balance. The notch also creates two new horizontal edges of 3 cm each, adding 6 cm total. So the new perimeter is 52 + 6 = 58 cm. 

52 cm is wrong because the notch does change the perimeter. 56 cm is wrong because it adds only one new edge instead of both new 3 cm edges. 66 cm is wrong because the 4 cm depth should not increase the perimeter overall when the removed and added vertical sides are compared.

questionText: A U-shaped snack path is made from a 15 m by 9 m rectangle with a top notch 3 m wide and 4 m deep. Why is the calculation 15 + 9 + 3 + 4 + 3 + 4 + 15 + 9 = 62 m incorrect?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Measurement & Conversion: Perimeter & Area, aligned to Students can find the perimeter of rectilinear shapes.
options: [{"text":"It counts the full top edge as 15 m and also counts the 3 m bottom of the notch, but the full top 15 m is no longer one outside edge after the notch is cut.","correct":true},{"text":"It should not count the two 4 m notch sides because edges inside the shape are never part of the perimeter.","correct":false},{"text":"It forgets that the left and right sides of 9 m should each be counted twice.","correct":false},{"text":"Any notch must make the perimeter smaller than the rectangle perimeter, so 62 m cannot be correct.","correct":false}]
explanation:
The error is in the top edge. After a 3 m wide notch is cut from the top, the shape no longer has one full top edge of 15 m. Instead, the top is split into two outside pieces, and the 3 m bottom of the notch is part of the boundary. Counting 15 m and also counting 3 m overcounts the top structure. 

The two 4 m notch sides are part of the perimeter because they are on the boundary of the shape. The 9 m left and right sides are each counted once, not twice. A notch does not always make the perimeter smaller; in this case it increases because extra edges are created.
